subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Adverse Possession: Limitation Periods, Factual Possession, Animus Possidendi, and the Irish Position

questions.question: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, what is the limitation period for an action to recover unregistered private land?

options.A: Six years
options.B: Twelve years
options.C: Twenty years
options.D: Thirty years

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, the limitation period for an action to recover unregistered private land is twelve years. Once a squatter has been in adverse possession for twelve years, the paper owner's right of action to recover the land is extinguished.

questions.question: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, what is the limitation period for an action by the State to recover land?

options.A: Twelve years
options.B: Twenty years
options.C: Thirty years
options.D: Forty years

questions.explanation: The Statute of Limitations 1957 provides a limitation period of thirty years for actions by the State to recover land. The extended period reflects the State's special position as landowner and its need for additional time to protect the public interest in State land.

questions.question: The two essential elements required to establish adverse possession are:

options.A: Registered title and continuous occupation
options.B: Factual possession and animus possidendi (intention to possess)
options.C: Physical enclosure and payment of rates
options.D: Nec vi and nec clam only

questions.explanation: Adverse possession requires two essential elements: (1) factual possession — the squatter must be in factual control of the land, exercising the type of possession that an owner would; and (2) animus possidendi — the intention to possess the land as if one were its owner. Both elements must be established.

questions.question: In Powell v McFarlane (1977), Slade J identified the key elements of factual possession in adverse possession. Which of the following is NOT part of that formulation?

options.A: The squatter must be dealing with the land as an occupying owner might be expected to deal with it
options.B: The acts of possession must be open and not secret
options.C: The squatter must have the permission of the paper title owner
options.D: The squatter must have exclusive possession of the land

questions.explanation: In Powell v McFarlane (1977), Slade J formulated the requirements for factual possession: dealing with the land as an occupying owner; open, not secret possession; and exclusive possession. Permission from the paper owner is antithetical to adverse possession — if the squatter possesses with the owner's permission, the possession is not adverse.

questions.question: The Latin phrase 'animus possidendi' means:

options.A: The physical act of possessing land
options.B: The intention to possess land as an owner — the mental element of adverse possession
options.C: The acquisition of title through long use
options.D: The obligation to register adverse possession within twelve years

questions.explanation: Animus possidendi literally means 'intention to possess.' In adverse possession, it refers to the squatter's intention to possess the land as an owner would — not merely to use or occupy it temporarily. This mental element must accompany the physical acts of possession to establish adverse possession.

questions.question: In JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v Graham [2003] (House of Lords), what was the outcome in relation to the adverse possession claim?

options.A: The squatter's claim failed because Pye's paper title was protected by registration
options.B: The squatter's (Graham's) adverse possession claim succeeded — the Grahams had been in factual possession with the necessary animus possidendi for the required period
options.C: The House of Lords held that grazing of land was insufficient to constitute factual possession
options.D: The claim failed because the Grahams had previously paid rent and so had been in licensed occupation

questions.explanation: In JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v Graham [2003] HL, the House of Lords upheld the Grahams' adverse possession claim. They had been in factual possession of the disputed field (grazing cattle, farming it) and had the necessary animus possidendi, for the required twelve-year period. Pye's right of action had been extinguished.

questions.question: In Buckinghamshire County Council v Moran [1990] (Court of Appeal), which act was held to be sufficiently strong evidence of animus possidendi?

options.A: Depositing rubbish on the disputed plot
options.B: Walking across the land regularly
options.C: Incorporating the disputed plot into the squatter's own garden by locking the gate to it
options.D: Erecting a temporary fence along one side of the disputed land

questions.explanation: In Buckinghamshire County Council v Moran [1990] CA, Mr Moran's act of incorporating the disputed plot into his own garden — cultivating it and locking the gate so that he had exclusive access — was held to be sufficiently strong evidence of animus possidendi. The court found that he intended to possess the land as owner, regardless of any intention to yield to the true owner if required.

questions.question: The three requirements often associated with establishing adverse possession are that possession must be 'nec vi, nec clam, nec precario.' What do these Latin phrases mean?

options.A: Not by fraud, not by registration, not by contract
options.B: Not by force, not secretly, not by permission
options.C: Not on State land, not on registered land, not on agricultural land
options.D: Not by a tenant, not by a licensee, not by a mortgagee

questions.explanation: Nec vi, nec clam, nec precario means: 'not by force, not secretly, not by permission.' For adverse possession to run, the squatter's possession must not have been taken by force (vi), must not be hidden or clandestine (clam), and must not be with the permission (precario) of the owner. If possession is permitted by the owner, it is not adverse.

questions.question: If the paper owner of land gives the squatter written permission to use the land, what is the effect on any adverse possession claim?

options.A: The permission is irrelevant — adverse possession can run even with the owner's consent
options.B: The permission confirms the squatter's possessory title and accelerates the twelve-year period
options.C: The permission interrupts the running of time — the squatter's possession becomes precario and cannot be adverse while permission subsists
options.D: The permission transfers the paper title to the squatter immediately

questions.explanation: If the paper owner gives the squatter permission to occupy or use the land, the squatter's possession becomes precario (permissive). Permissive possession is not adverse possession — the limitation period does not run. Time only begins to run again if the permission is revoked and the squatter continues in factual possession with animus possidendi.

questions.question: A squatter adversely possesses unregistered private land for ten years, then vacates the land for two years before returning. What is the effect on the limitation period?

options.A: The ten years of previous possession is lost — time starts again from scratch on return
options.B: The previous ten years is preserved — time resumes from where it left off
options.C: The abandonment defeats any adverse possession claim permanently
options.D: The limitation period is reduced to six years after a break in possession

questions.explanation: Adverse possession must be continuous. If the squatter vacates the land, the clock stops and the accrued period is generally lost. When the squatter returns, time starts again from the beginning. The squatter would need a fresh twelve-year period of continuous adverse possession. (Note: different rules may apply if the paper owner retakes possession and then loses it again.)

questions.question: In Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975] (Irish Supreme Court), the court considered what happens when a squatter adversely possesses land held on a lease. What did the court hold?

options.A: The squatter acquires the freehold interest — the lease is irrelevant
options.B: The squatter acquires the leasehold interest of the lessee, not the freehold — the squatter takes the lessee's position and remains subject to the lease terms
options.C: Adverse possession cannot run against leasehold land in Ireland
options.D: The squatter's title is defeated by the lessor's superior freehold title

questions.explanation: In Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975] SC, the Irish Supreme Court confirmed that when a squatter adversely possesses leasehold land, they acquire only the leasehold interest of the lessee (the paper title holder for the period of the lease). The squatter steps into the shoes of the lessee, subject to all the obligations under the lease. The freehold is unaffected — the squatter does not obtain the freeholder's title.

questions.question: Ireland has not adopted the reforms to adverse possession introduced by the Land Registration Act 2002 in England and Wales. What was the key change introduced by the 2002 Act in England?

options.A: The 2002 Act abolished adverse possession entirely in England and Wales
options.B: The 2002 Act reduced the limitation period from twelve to six years
options.C: The 2002 Act introduced a notification system for registered land — the paper owner is notified of the adverse possession claim and can defeat it by taking action within two years
options.D: The 2002 Act extended the limitation period for State land from thirty to sixty years

questions.explanation: The Land Registration Act 2002 (England and Wales) introduced a new regime for registered land: a squatter who has been in adverse possession for ten years can apply to be registered. The paper owner is then notified and has two years to bring proceedings to defeat the claim. If the owner does nothing, the squatter can apply again and will be registered. Ireland has not adopted this reform.

questions.question: In Powell v McFarlane (1977), Slade J emphasised that animus possidendi must be assessed objectively. What does this mean?

options.A: The squatter's private intention is irrelevant — only public registration matters
options.B: The squatter's intention is assessed from the outward, observable acts — not from their private uncommunicated beliefs
options.C: The paper owner's intention to reclaim the land is the primary test
options.D: Animus possidendi is presumed from any act of physical presence on the land

questions.explanation: In Powell v McFarlane, Slade J held that animus possidendi must be assessed objectively — from the squatter's outward acts and the circumstances as a whole. The squatter's private, uncommunicated intention is not enough. However, evidence of subjective intention is relevant to corroborate or contradict what is apparent from the outward acts.

questions.question: A squatter begins adversely possessing private unregistered land in 2011. In 2024, the paper owner discovers the squatter's occupation. Can the paper owner still recover the land?

options.A: Yes — the owner's discovery restarts the limitation period
options.B: No — thirteen years of adverse possession have elapsed; the twelve-year limitation period has run and the owner's right to recover is extinguished
options.C: Yes — the paper owner has a further twelve years from discovery to bring proceedings
options.D: No — but the owner is entitled to compensation from the squatter equal to the land's market value

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, the limitation period of twelve years runs from when the squatter's adverse possession began — not from when the owner discovers it. By 2024, thirteen years have elapsed since 2011. The paper owner's right of action to recover the land was extinguished in 2023, and mere discovery does not revive it.

questions.question: In JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v Graham [2003], the House of Lords rejected Pye's argument that the Grahams lacked animus possidendi because they would have given up the land if asked. The court held that:

options.A: Willingness to yield possession on request demonstrated that the Grahams were licensees, not possessors
options.B: Animus possidendi requires an intention to occupy in defiance of the true owner under all circumstances
options.C: The intention to yield to the true owner if required does not negate animus possidendi — the Grahams intended to possess the land as owners in the meantime
options.D: The Grahams' willingness to yield demonstrated bad faith, which defeated the adverse possession claim

questions.explanation: In Pye v Graham, the House of Lords held that the Grahams' willingness to vacate if Pye had asked did not negate animus possidendi. Animus possidendi requires an intention to possess the land as owner for the time being — it does not require an intention to resist the true owner's demands or to hold in defiance. The Grahams had the requisite intent.

questions.question: A person grazes sheep on a neighbouring field they do not own, without objection from the owner, for thirteen years. They believe the land belongs to them but have never paid rates or erected fences. Are they likely to establish adverse possession?

options.A: Yes — thirteen years of open use is sufficient without more
options.B: No — grazing sheep can never constitute factual possession for the purposes of adverse possession
options.C: Possibly — grazing may constitute factual possession, but the court would scrutinise whether the acts are sufficient given the nature of the land and whether animus possidendi is established
options.D: No — they must have erected fences and paid rates to establish adverse possession

questions.explanation: Grazing animals on open land may constitute factual possession, depending on the nature of the land and the extent of the use (as in Pye v Graham where cattle grazing sufficed). The court would assess whether the squatter's acts of grazing, combined with their animus possidendi, are sufficient given the character of the land. The absence of fences and rate payments is not automatically fatal.

questions.question: What is the effect on the limitation period of a written acknowledgment by the squatter of the paper owner's title?

options.A: A written acknowledgment has no effect on the limitation period
options.B: A written acknowledgment resets the limitation period — time starts running again from the date of acknowledgment
options.C: A written acknowledgment permanently defeats the adverse possession claim
options.D: A written acknowledgment converts the adverse possession into a licence

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, a written acknowledgment of the paper owner's title by the squatter (or their agent) resets the limitation period. Time starts running afresh from the date of acknowledgment. This is why paper owners who suspect adverse possession often seek a written acknowledgment from occupiers.

questions.question: A squatter fences off a section of a larger plot of land and farms it as their own for fourteen years. The paper owner owns the entire larger plot. What is the effect of the adverse possession?

options.A: The squatter acquires title to the entire larger plot, not just the fenced section
options.B: The squatter acquires title to the fenced section only — adverse possession is limited to the area actually possessed
options.C: The squatter must purchase the fenced section from the paper owner at market value
options.D: The squatter acquires a right to remain but not a title to the land

questions.explanation: Adverse possession operates only over the area actually possessed by the squatter. The limitation period runs against the paper owner only in respect of land of which the squatter has actually been in factual possession with animus possidendi. The paper owner's title to the remainder of the larger plot is unaffected.

questions.question: In Powell v McFarlane (1977), why did the claimant's adverse possession claim ultimately fail despite years of use?

options.A: The claimant had paid rent to the owner at some point during the period
options.B: The court found that the acts relied upon (cutting grass, grazing a cow) were not, in their context, sufficient to establish factual possession as owner — they were equivocal acts
options.C: The limitation period had not yet expired when the proceedings were brought
options.D: The claimant had acknowledged the owner's title in writing

questions.explanation: In Powell v McFarlane, Slade J held that the claimant's acts — principally grazing a cow and cutting grass as a teenager — were equivocal and did not, in the particular context, establish the degree of factual possession required. The acts needed to be unequivocal evidence of the squatter treating the land as their own. Merely using land in a minor way does not suffice.

questions.question: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, when does the limitation period begin to run in favour of a squatter?

options.A: When the squatter first sets foot on the land
options.B: When the squatter has been in possession for six months continuously
options.C: When the paper owner's right of action to recover the land first accrues — generally when the squatter first takes adverse possession
options.D: When the squatter registers their interest in the Land Registry

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, time begins to run when the paper owner's right of action to recover the land first accrues — which is generally when the squatter first takes adverse possession of the land. It is from this point that the twelve-year period (or thirty-year period for State land) runs.

questions.question: A squatter occupies private unregistered land for twelve years. The paper owner's right of action is extinguished. What happens to the title to the land?

options.A: The paper owner retains title but loses the right to sue for possession
options.B: The land escheats to the State
options.C: The paper owner's title is extinguished and the squatter holds the best available title — a possessory freehold
options.D: The squatter automatically becomes the registered owner of the land

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, once the limitation period expires, the paper owner's title to the land is extinguished (not merely barred). The squatter holds the best available title — a possessory freehold — which is good against all except those with a prior and still-subsisting right. The squatter does not automatically become the registered owner but can apply for registration.

questions.question: Can a squatter 'tack' (add together) their own period of adverse possession with that of a previous squatter?

options.A: No — each squatter must establish their own independent twelve-year period from scratch
options.B: Yes — successive periods of adverse possession by different squatters can be aggregated, provided there is continuity of possession between them
options.C: Only if the previous squatter formally transferred their possessory title to the current squatter by deed
options.D: Only with the paper owner's written consent

questions.explanation: It is possible to 'tack' successive periods of adverse possession. Where squatter A is in adverse possession for seven years, then transfers their possessory interest to squatter B who continues for a further five years, B can aggregate both periods to reach the twelve-year total — provided there was continuity of possession between the two squatters.

questions.question: In Buckinghamshire County Council v Moran [1990], why did Mr Moran succeed in his adverse possession claim against the council?

options.A: He had paid rates on the land for twelve years
options.B: He had a written licence from the council to use the land
options.C: He had incorporated the disputed plot into his garden, locking the gate and treating it as his own — clearly establishing factual possession and animus possidendi for over twelve years
options.D: The council's use of the land for road-widening was a future contingent use only, so their title was not protected

questions.explanation: Mr Moran succeeded because he had, for over twelve years, incorporated the disputed plot into his garden, cultivated it, and maintained a locked gate — thereby demonstrating both factual possession (dealing with the land as an owner) and animus possidendi (intending to possess it as owner). The council's paper title and future plans did not defeat the completed adverse possession.

questions.question: If a tenant adversely possesses land belonging to their landlord, can time run against the landlord under the Statute of Limitations 1957?

options.A: Yes — time runs against the landlord from the moment the tenant takes adverse possession of the landlord's land
options.B: No — a tenant's possession is always deemed to be the landlord's possession; the landlord-tenant relationship prevents adverse possession
options.C: Only if the tenancy has been formally surrendered beforehand
options.D: Only if the landlord fails to collect rent for twelve years

questions.explanation: A tenant's possession of demised premises is not adverse to the landlord — it is permissive, held under the lease. The nec precario requirement means that permissive possession cannot be adverse. Time runs against the landlord only if the tenant's possession becomes truly adverse — e.g., if the tenant claims land beyond the demised premises or if the lease has expired and the tenant holds over without permission.

questions.question: The effect of adverse possession on a leasehold estate (per Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975]) means that a squatter who adversely possesses leasehold land:

options.A: Acquires the freehold title and the lease is merged
options.B: Acquires only the leasehold interest — standing in the shoes of the former lessee and bound by the lease covenants for its remaining term
options.C: Takes the land free of all obligations including the lease covenants
options.D: Must obtain the landlord's consent before their possessory title is recognised

questions.explanation: In Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975] SC, the Supreme Court held that a squatter who adversely possesses leasehold land steps into the shoes of the lessee. The squatter acquires the leasehold interest only, taking the land subject to all existing lease covenants. The freeholder's title is unaffected — the squatter does not acquire the freehold.

questions.question: A landowner erects a sign saying 'This land is owned by X Ltd — any trespassers will be prosecuted.' Does this sign prevent adverse possession from running?

options.A: Yes — erecting a sign is a formal assertion of title that prevents time from running
options.B: No — erecting a sign does not constitute a sufficient act to interrupt the squatter's adverse possession, which requires the owner to take physical steps to retake possession or bring legal proceedings
options.C: Yes — the squatter is put on notice and their possession becomes permissive
options.D: No — but it reduces the limitation period to six years

questions.explanation: Simply erecting a sign does not interrupt adverse possession or restart the limitation period. To interrupt adverse possession, the paper owner must take legal proceedings to recover the land or physically retake possession. The sign does not constitute the latter. Adverse possession runs on regardless of the owner's knowledge or warnings.

questions.question: What is the effect of accrual of the right of action (and thus the start of the limitation period) where land is owned by a minor (person under 18)?

options.A: The limitation period cannot run against a minor — it begins only when they reach eighteen
options.B: The period runs from when the right accrues, regardless of the landowner's age
options.C: The limitation period is extended to twenty-four years where the owner is a minor
options.D: The Statute of Limitations 1957 provides a disability exception — time does not run during the minority of the owner

questions.explanation: The Statute of Limitations 1957 contains disability provisions. If the paper owner is under a disability (including minority) when the right of action first accrues, the limitation period does not run until the disability ceases. This protects vulnerable landowners from losing their land through adverse possession during periods of incapacity.

questions.question: In the context of adverse possession, 'factual possession' requires that the squatter:

options.A: Has a formal legal document evidencing their claim to possession
options.B: Has been in receipt of rents and profits from the land during the limitation period
options.C: Has been in physical control of the land, dealing with it in the way an owner would — exercising control in a manner appropriate to the type and character of the land
options.D: Has registered a caution or inhibition against the paper title

questions.explanation: Factual possession requires that the squatter exercises physical control over the land in a manner appropriate to its character — farming agricultural land, maintaining a garden, building on development land. The squatter must deal with the land as an occupying owner would. The nature and extent of the acts required will depend on the character and location of the land.

questions.question: A squatter enters adverse possession of private unregistered land in 2010. The paper owner discovers the encroachment in 2015 but takes no action. In 2023, the owner brings proceedings to recover the land. Can the owner succeed?

options.A: Yes — the owner brought proceedings within twelve years of discovering the encroachment in 2015
options.B: No — the twelve-year limitation period from the start of adverse possession (2010) expired in 2022; the owner's right of action is extinguished
options.C: Yes — the limitation period cannot expire while the owner is in ignorance of the squatter's presence
options.D: It depends on whether the squatter had paid rates during the period

questions.explanation: The limitation period under the Statute of Limitations 1957 runs from the date of adverse possession (2010), not from the date of discovery. The twelve-year period expired in 2022. By 2023, the owner's right of action has been extinguished. Discovery of the encroachment does not restart or extend the limitation period.

questions.question: The human rights dimension of adverse possession was considered in JA Pye (Oxford) Ltd v United Kingdom [2007] (European Court of Human Rights Grand Chamber). The Grand Chamber ultimately held that:

options.A: Adverse possession is incompatible with the right to property under Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR
options.B: The English adverse possession regime as it then stood was compatible with Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR — the UK enjoyed a margin of appreciation in regulating property rights, and the twelve-year rule pursued a legitimate aim proportionately
options.C: The UK was required to compensate Pye for the loss of its land to the Grahams
options.D: The Grand Chamber referred the case to the UK Supreme Court for determination

questions.explanation: The Grand Chamber of the ECtHR held in Pye v UK [2007] that the English adverse possession regime was compatible with Article 1 Protocol 1 ECHR. States enjoy a margin of appreciation in regulating property rights, and the limitation rules (pursued by the UK) pursued legitimate policy aims (legal certainty, efficient land use) proportionately. There was no violation.

questions.question: A squatter occupies private unregistered land for twelve years. They then sell their possessory interest to a purchaser. Can the purchaser enforce the possessory title?

options.A: No — a squatter cannot sell or transfer a possessory title
options.B: Yes — a possessory title acquired by adverse possession is transmissible inter vivos and by succession, and the purchaser takes the benefit of it
options.C: Only if the original paper owner consents to the transfer
options.D: Only if the possessory title is first registered in the Land Registry

questions.explanation: A possessory title acquired through adverse possession is a valid property interest that can be transferred inter vivos by sale or otherwise, or transmitted on death. The purchaser of the possessory interest takes the benefit of the extinguished paper title and can resist any claim by the original paper owner.

questions.question: What is the relevance of 'nec vi' (not by force) to adverse possession?

options.A: If possession was taken by force, time runs at double the normal rate
options.B: Force must be used to establish adverse possession against a reluctant landowner
options.C: If possession was taken by force, the squatter's possession is not 'peaceful' and may not qualify as adverse — though in practice the landowner's remedy is to bring proceedings promptly
options.D: Possession taken by force is void under the criminal law and cannot found a limitation period claim

questions.explanation: The requirement that possession not be by force (nec vi) means that adverse possession should be peaceful — not taken by actual violence or persistent defiance of the owner's attempts to retake the land. If possession is forcibly taken and maintained against the owner's active resistance, this may affect the character of possession. In practice, the owner's remedy is to bring proceedings within the limitation period.

questions.question: A squatter adversely possesses land for nine years, then vacates the land. Six months later, the paper owner attempts to retake possession. Three years later, the squatter re-enters and resumes possession. From when does the new limitation period run?

options.A: From the original start of possession — the nine years is still counted
options.B: From the squatter's re-entry — a fresh twelve-year period begins, with the previous nine years lost
options.C: From the paper owner's attempt to retake possession
options.D: From the date the squatter first vacated the land

questions.explanation: Where a squatter vacates land before the limitation period has run, the accrued period of adverse possession is generally lost. On re-entry, a fresh limitation period begins. The squatter must start again and complete a fresh twelve-year period of continuous adverse possession. The previous nine years cannot be counted towards the new period.

questions.question: Under the Irish legal framework (as at 2024), has Ireland adopted the Land Registration Act 2002 notification regime for adverse possession of registered land?

options.A: Yes — Ireland adopted the 2002 regime by statutory instrument in 2010
options.B: No — Ireland has retained the traditional Statute of Limitations approach and has not adopted the notification regime
options.C: Yes — but only for residential properties
options.D: Ireland has its own separate notification regime under the Registration of Title Act 1964

questions.explanation: Ireland has not adopted the Land Registration Act 2002 reforms introduced in England and Wales. The traditional Statute of Limitations 1957 approach applies in Ireland — the limitation period runs and, on expiry, the paper owner's title is extinguished regardless of notification. There is no Irish equivalent of the two-year objection window introduced in England.

questions.question: A squatter adversely possesses registered land in Ireland for twelve years. What must the squatter do to have their title formally recognised?

options.A: Nothing — the limitation period automatically vests title in the squatter
options.B: Apply to the Land Registry to be registered as owner on the basis of their adverse possession, providing evidence of the requisite period of possession
options.C: Obtain an order from the High Court declaring them the owner
options.D: Purchase the land from the registered owner at a price fixed by the court

questions.explanation: Although the squatter's adverse possession extinguishes the registered owner's title after twelve years, the squatter must apply to the Land Registry to have their interest formally recorded. The squatter must provide evidence of the adverse possession. Formal registration provides certainty and enables the squatter to deal with the land.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why the adverse possession doctrine exists as a matter of policy?

options.A: To benefit trespassers at the expense of landowners
options.B: To incentivise active use of land, extinguish stale claims, achieve certainty of title, and prevent the perpetuation of disputes about title to land long after evidence has become unreliable
options.C: To allow the State to reclaim unused land for public purposes
options.D: To reward those who improve or develop land regardless of ownership

questions.explanation: The policy rationale for adverse possession includes: (1) incentivising active use and management of land; (2) achieving certainty of title by extinguishing stale claims; (3) preventing hardship to those who have relied on possession for many years; and (4) avoiding disputes based on evidence that has become unreliable over long periods. These policies balance the interests of landowners and possessors.

questions.question: A squatter adversely possesses a strip of land adjoining their garden for fifteen years. The strip was part of the neighbour's registered land. The registered owner attempts to recover. What is the outcome?

options.A: The registered owner recovers — registration provides absolute title that cannot be defeated by adverse possession
options.B: The squatter prevails — the twelve-year limitation period has run and the registered owner's right is extinguished
options.C: The registered owner has twelve years from the date of the squatter's registration application to object
options.D: The squatter must pay the owner the value of the strip before they can assert their title

questions.explanation: In Ireland (unlike in England and Wales under the Land Registration Act 2002), the registration of the paper title does not provide absolute protection against adverse possession. The Statute of Limitations 1957 applies to registered land. After twelve years of adverse possession, the registered owner's title is extinguished and the squatter's possessory title prevails.

questions.question: The concept of 'paper title' in the context of adverse possession means:

options.A: A title that has been typed and printed rather than handwritten
options.B: The documentary title of the owner — the chain of ownership deeds showing formal legal ownership, as opposed to possessory title acquired through adverse possession
options.C: A temporary title granted pending completion of a conveyance
options.D: Title registered in the Land Registry in paper form

questions.explanation: The 'paper title' or 'documentary title' refers to the formal chain of title deeds showing legal ownership. It is contrasted with possessory or de facto title acquired through adverse possession. In adverse possession cases, the paper title holder and the possessor are different people, and the question is whether the limitation period has extinguished the paper owner's title.

questions.question: A farmer adversely possesses a field for ten years. The field's paper owner then grants a lease of the field to a third party. What is the effect on the farmer's adverse possession?

options.A: The lease defeats the farmer's adverse possession claim entirely
options.B: The lease constitutes an act of the paper owner that interrupts the farmer's adverse possession, restarting the limitation period
options.C: The lease has no effect on the running of time — the farmer's adverse possession continues against both the paper owner and the lessee
options.D: The farmer's adverse possession transfers automatically to the leasehold interest

questions.explanation: Where the paper owner takes a positive act in relation to the land — such as granting a lease — this may constitute an act that interrupts the squatter's adverse possession and restarts the limitation period. The grant of a lease asserts the owner's dominion over the land and may interrupt the running of time. The position depends on whether the act is sufficient to interrupt possession.

questions.question: An FE-1 exam question asks about a squatter who uses an uncultivated field for twelve years, occasionally cutting grass and walking across it. The paper owner now claims possession. What is the most likely outcome?

options.A: The squatter succeeds — twelve years of any use is sufficient
options.B: The paper owner succeeds — occasional grass cutting and walking are equivocal and insufficient to establish factual possession of the type required for adverse possession
options.C: The squatter succeeds — the paper owner's inaction for twelve years estops them from recovering
options.D: The outcome depends on whether the squatter paid rates on the field

questions.explanation: Applying Powell v McFarlane, occasional grass cutting and walking across land are equivocal acts that may not suffice as factual possession, particularly if the land is uncultivated and if the acts could be consistent with something other than ownership. The court would require clear and unequivocal acts demonstrating that the squatter was treating the land as their own — the facts here are likely insufficient.

questions.question: If the squatter in Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975] had occupied the land after the lease had expired and no new lease had been granted, who would the squatter have dispossessed?

options.A: The lessee only — the freeholder's title would remain unaffected
options.B: The freeholder, as without a current lease the freeholder would be in constructive possession of the land
options.C: Both the lessee and the freeholder simultaneously
options.D: No one — adverse possession cannot begin after lease expiry

questions.explanation: Once a lease expires and no new lease is granted, the freeholder's right to possession revives. If a squatter then adversely possesses the land, they would be dispossessing the freeholder (not a lessee). On expiry of the twelve-year period, the freeholder's right of action would be extinguished and the squatter would acquire a possessory fee simple.

questions.question: Which of the following acts would most clearly establish factual possession in adverse possession law?

options.A: Expressing a verbal intention to claim the land as one's own
options.B: Occasionally walking across the land without using it for any other purpose
options.C: Fencing off the land, cultivating it, and excluding all others including the paper owner for the required limitation period
options.D: Paying insurance on the land or insuring the buildings on it

questions.explanation: Fencing off land, cultivating it, and excluding all others — including the paper owner — are classic acts of factual possession. They demonstrate the squatter is dealing with the land as an occupying owner would, exercising exclusive control consistent with ownership. These are the clearest indicators of adverse possession.

questions.question: A landowner with a right of action to recover land dies before the twelve-year period expires. Does the limitation period continue to run against their estate?

options.A: No — the death of the paper owner automatically resets the limitation period
options.B: Yes — the limitation period continues to run against the estate; the personal representative must take action within the remaining time
options.C: The limitation period is suspended for three years after the paper owner's death
options.D: The limitation period cannot expire within two years of the paper owner's death

questions.explanation: The limitation period continues to run against the estate of a deceased paper owner. The personal representative steps into the shoes of the deceased and must bring proceedings to recover the land within the remaining period of limitation. Death does not reset or suspend the period unless the disability provisions apply.

questions.question: In what respect does Ireland's adverse possession regime differ significantly from the post-2002 English registered land regime?

options.A: Ireland requires adverse possession to be continuous; England does not
options.B: Ireland applies adverse possession to leasehold land; England does not
options.C: Ireland still extinguishes the paper owner's title automatically after twelve years without notification; England's 2002 Act introduced a notification system giving registered owners an opportunity to resist adverse possession claims
options.D: Ireland requires registration of adverse possession claims before they can succeed; England does not

questions.explanation: The key difference is the notification system. Under the Land Registration Act 2002 (England and Wales), a squatter of registered land who has possessed for ten years can apply for registration, but the registered owner is notified and has two years to object. Ireland has retained the Statute of Limitations 1957 approach, under which the paper owner's title is extinguished automatically after twelve years without any notification procedure.

questions.question: A squatter enters adverse possession of land in 2008. In 2016 (before the limitation period expires), the paper owner brings proceedings to recover the land. What is the effect of bringing proceedings?

options.A: The proceedings are irrelevant — time continues to run regardless
options.B: The bringing of proceedings interrupts the running of time — if the owner succeeds in the proceedings, the squatter's possessory claim is defeated
options.C: The proceedings convert the squatter's interest into a licence
options.D: The proceedings must be concluded within two years or they are automatically dismissed

questions.explanation: The bringing of legal proceedings by the paper owner to recover land is an act that interrupts the running of time. If the owner brings proceedings before the twelve-year period expires and successfully recovers the land, the squatter's adverse possession claim is defeated. The squatter's period of possession does not count in their favour if the owner takes timely action.

questions.question: A squatter occupies land bordering a river. Over the period of limitation, the river gradually changes course so that the squatter's land area increases. Is the squatter's adverse possession claim affected by this natural change?

options.A: Yes — the squatter must re-establish adverse possession over the new area from the date it accreted
options.B: No — natural accretion follows the land and the squatter's adverse possession covers the accreted area automatically
options.C: Yes — the squatter loses their claim over the original area due to the change in the river course
options.D: The paper owner automatically acquires the accreted area regardless of the squatter's possession

questions.explanation: Natural accretion — the gradual addition of land by natural processes (e.g., river or sea deposits) — generally follows the ownership of the adjoining land. Where a squatter is in adverse possession of the adjoining land, natural accretion to that land during the period of adverse possession would also form part of the squatter's possessory interest. The squatter does not need to establish separate adverse possession over accreted land.

questions.question: An Irish property law student is asked to advise a client who has been using an unused plot of land next to their house for thirteen years, maintaining it as a garden extension. The paper owner now seeks recovery. The most accurate advice is:

options.A: The paper owner will succeed — using land as a garden extension is too trivial to constitute adverse possession
options.B: The client has a strong adverse possession claim — thirteen years of open, continuous possession as a garden extension, combined with the necessary animus possidendi, satisfies the Statute of Limitations 1957 twelve-year period; the paper owner's right of action is likely extinguished
options.C: The client should offer to purchase the plot as adverse possession does not apply to residential land
options.D: The client must register their adverse possession claim before they can assert it against the paper owner

questions.explanation: Using an adjacent plot as a garden extension for thirteen years — cultivating it, treating it as part of one's own property — is capable of establishing both factual possession and animus possidendi (see Buckinghamshire CC v Moran). Thirteen years exceeds the twelve-year limitation period under the Statute of Limitations 1957. The client has a strong adverse possession claim and the paper owner's right is likely extinguished.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the disability provisions in the Statute of Limitations 1957 is correct?

options.A: If the paper owner becomes mentally incapacitated after the limitation period has started running, time is suspended from the date of incapacity
options.B: The disability provisions only apply if the paper owner was under a disability at the time the right of action first accrued — a subsequent disability does not suspend the running of time
options.C: Mental incapacity at any time during the limitation period extends the period by six additional years
options.D: The disability provisions apply to both the squatter and the paper owner equally

questions.explanation: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957, the disability provisions apply where the person entitled to bring the action is under a disability (e.g., a minor or of unsound mind) when the right of action first accrues. A disability that arises after time has begun to run does not suspend the period. The disability must exist at the outset — from the moment the right of action first accrues.

questions.question: The key difference between 'adverse possession' and mere 'trespass' is that:

options.A: Adverse possession is only possible on unregistered land; trespass applies to both registered and unregistered land
options.B: Adverse possession requires continuous, exclusive factual possession with animus possidendi for the full limitation period — a single act of trespass creates no title and does not start the limitation period running
options.C: A trespasser automatically acquires a licence after one year of occupation
options.D: Adverse possession requires court sanction; trespass does not

questions.explanation: Adverse possession requires sustained, continuous, exclusive factual possession for the full limitation period, combined with animus possidendi throughout. A single trespass, or even intermittent trespass, does not start the limitation period running and creates no rights. Adverse possession is a long-term, sustained claim that eventually extinguishes the paper owner's title.

questions.question: For an FE-1 exam question on adverse possession, which combination of authorities would be most important to cite?

options.A: Walsh v Lonsdale (1882); Spencer's Case (1583); Street v Mountford [1985]
options.B: Statute of Limitations 1957; Powell v McFarlane (1977); JA Pye v Graham [2003]; Buckinghamshire CC v Moran [1990]; Perry v Woodfarm Homes [1975]
options.C: Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995]; Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]; Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011]
options.D: Asher v Whitlock (1865); Armory v Delamirie (1722); Re Ellenborough Park [1956]

questions.explanation: The core adverse possession authorities are: the Statute of Limitations 1957 (limitation periods); Powell v McFarlane (factual possession and animus possidendi elements); JA Pye v Graham (application of the elements — grazing); Buckinghamshire CC v Moran (animus possidendi — garden extension); and Perry v Woodfarm Homes (Irish Supreme Court — squatter on leasehold takes leasehold interest). These are the essential citations for any adverse possession FE-1 answer.
